observation: During our exploration of the sense of smell, they actively participated in a sensory activity where they matched different scents to corresponding cards, strengthening their observation and identification skills. They also enjoyed creating a model "nose" using condiments of their choice, thoughtfully selecting scents based on personal preference. Through discussion, they developed an understanding of the close connection between smell and taste and how the absence of one sense can influence overall sensory experience.

He is developing strong problem-solving and engineering skills by building structures using simple materials like spaghetti, marshmallows, and Magna-Tiles. He can observe how smaller parts come together to form a stable dome and explore how shapes and connections affect a structure's strength.

During our exploration of the sense of sight, they showed great curiosity and engagement while investigating optical illusions and using special goggles to understand how different animals see the world. They were excited to discover why animals perceive their surroundings differently from humans and asked thoughtful questions about how our eyes can sometimes "trick" us. Through these activities, they began developing an understanding of the biological processes behind vision and how perception works.

During our exploration of the sense of taste, they participated in a taste test to identify different flavors and reflect on their sensory experiences. They created a visual model of the tongue and learned about taste receptors, discussing how certain areas of the tongue may be more sensitive to specific flavors, while understanding that all taste qualities can be detected across the tongue. Through these activities, they strengthened their understanding of how taste works and how it connects to other senses.

During our exploration of the sense of hearing, they learned about the structure and function of the ear, identifying its main parts and understanding how sound travels through them. Through interactive "guess the sound" activities and a hearing test experiment, they explored how sound frequency (measured in hertz) affects what children and adults are able to hear. These hands-on investigations helped deepen their understanding of how hearing works and why hearing ability can vary between individuals.

During our exploration of the sense of touch, they learned how the skin functions as the body's largest organ and how it helps us detect different sensations such as temperature, pressure, and texture. Through hands-on activities, including creating handprints and footprints using various materials, they explored how different surfaces feel and how our skin responds to these stimuli. These experiences supported their understanding of how the sense of touch helps us interact safely and meaningfully with the world around us.

He showed great curiosity while exploring the food pyramid and learning about what kinds of foods are healthier to eat more often and which to enjoy less frequently. He was excited to try new flavors and recipes and saw the value of making healthy choices in daily life. During cooking sessions, he took leadership by reading the recipe steps aloud, guiding others in the process, and confidently applying math skills to measure quantities. His enthusiasm for cooking and understanding nutrition demonstrated a growing awareness of how food supports his overall development and well-being.

He actively engaged in hands-on investigations to explore how electricity works. Through trial and error, he successfully built simple circuits to light an LED or power a motor, showing curiosity, persistence, and a growing understanding of energy transfer. This experience helped strengthen both his scientific thinking and problem-solving skills.

He explored geometry by measuring the sides and angles of the triangles used in their dome structure. This hands-on activity deepened his understanding of shapes, measurement, and how geometry is used in real-world structures.Measures most side lengths and angles with some guidance. Demonstrates a growing understanding of triangle properties and measurement tools

He is beginning to observe and describe how structures found in nature, such as beehives and pinecones, can inspire human design. He is developing an understanding of biomimicry by identifying patterns and shapes in the natural world and thinking about how these ideas might be used in real-world solutions."

Throughout the week we played a game where the Lower Middle crew split into different teams (randomly or by their own choice). We exercised strategic thinking, patience, following the rules, collaboration with teammates, and other skills such as unpredictability and tension. Each team had a specific color, and they should not let other teams know their color. On the table, each team had nine lives; they had to take out two lives and put one back. It is a strategy game about hiding your own intentions in order not to be knocked out of the game. Most students told me that the game was fun and exciting, but some said it was stressful and intense. Overall, the Lower Middle showed that they can collaborate and compete without getting upset. I saw them engaged in the game, especially in the first rounds.

He is recognizing and using geometric shapes such as triangles, squares, and hexagons to build 3D structures. Through hands-on projects like dome building, he is learning how shapes fit and connect to create strong, balanced designs

Crew Meet was a great opportunity to practice focus skills with the Upper Primary crew. We played a game called "Hocus Pocus, Everybody Focus." We had also played it last week, and we repeated it this week to see if there were any improvements. From my perspective, the children were more focused than last week. All members of the crew participated, and at the end they told me that the game was both playful and useful. Together, we acknowledged that focus is an important skill that helps us beyond school.
Today in class they were exploring equivalent fractions with coloured blocks and applying their findings to answer questions related to equivalent fractions.

Today the Upper Primary Crew exercised their independent learning skills when working together analysing data that compared food waste per capita in various countries around the world. They needed to apply their knowledge of fractions, and apply basic numeracy skills to a real world context effectively.

He enjoyed walking through and understanding the hero's journey and found it exciting to write his own book following it. He was eager to bring in new characters, twists and the big challenge where good won over bad.

The learners from the upper primary crew were deeply engaged in exploring even and odd numbers through play. By using body movements and Lego blocks, they successfully identified even and odd patterns and discovered the rules for adding and subtracting even and odd numbers. They remained fully present, enthusiastic, and eager to delve deeper into their mathematical discoveries.

During the folder-making activity, the learners were engaged and showed great interest. They were able to give and receive constructive feedback, felt happy to share with their classmates, and were proud to present their work to their peers.

Teamwork and responsibility stood out as learners helped one another hold pieces steady and checked their work before attaching parts. With reminders about safety goggles and proper tool use, they were able to put the pieces together successfully, finishing their projects with growing confidence.
They worked systematically to look for patters when counting in 6's, 7's, and 9's by writing down as many multiples as they could and writing a description of the patter that they found.

Our learner can divide two-digit numbers, relate division to multiplication, identify the factors and factor pairs of a number, find the multiples of a given number up to 12, distinguish between prime and composite numbers up to 100, perform prime factorization and evaluate powers of 10.

The children painted a cauliflower to represent the sensory hemispheres of the brain, using color coding to highlight different senses. They carefully labeled each area to show where hearing, touch, sight, smell, and taste are processed, creating a visual and interactive model that links the brain to our senses.
The learner was attentive during the guided tour at the National Museum in Alba Iulia. The student asked numerous insightful questions and proactively sought help when needed.
Clearly understands synonyms and can explain that they are words with similar meanings.

Children worked carefully as they prepared the wood, sanding the edges to make them smooth and safe to handle. They showed patience and persistence while pounding nails and screwing in screws, practicing safe handling of tools and learning that slow, steady movements are more effective than rushing.

She explored renewable energy by installing a solar panel and connecting it to an LED light in a model house. Through this hands-on project, she discovered how solar panels collect energy during the day and power a light at night, building an early understanding of sustainability and energy transfer.

Learners explored place value using number cubes, dominoes, counting sticks, and wooden circles to visualize how numbers are made of tens and ones. Through hands-on activities, they practiced representing numbers with manipulatives and guessing the correct quantity when hearing a number, strengthening their understanding of how digits work together to form a number.

During our counting activities, learners practiced extending number sequences by writing the next five numbers after a given one on their whiteboards. They also identified and filled in missing numbers to strengthen their understanding of number order and patterns. This skill was reinforced through interactive practice in IXL, supporting accuracy and confidence in counting within 100.

Learners practiced building number fluency by counting to 100 in both Romanian and English and exploring number sequences. Through identifying what comes before and after given numbers, they strengthened their understanding of number order and developed greater confidence in recognizing patterns within the number system.

Learners were introduced to skip counting by 2s, 5s, and 10s to build their understanding of number patterns and grouping. They practiced this skill through real-world activities and reinforced their learning using IXL, developing fluency and confidence in recognizing counting sequences.

In mathematics today, the learners were introduced to adding fractions by using a model related shading parts of a circle. They then had to used the models to add together fractions of increasing complexity.

While working on the cave drape project, the children were excited to see and use the sewing machine. Even with all the excitement, they followed the safety rules very responsibly. Their respectful cooperation and seriousness showed excellent self-regulation, teamwork, and responsibility, all especially impressive for such young learners.
When a member of the crew had lost something that was very important to them they showed a willingness to explore high and low to find the item, helping their friend to calm down.

The children learned about opposites through a catchy song and were quick to understand the concept. Each child chose four opposite pairs and tried singing them alone. They began shyly but gained confidence as they saw our encouragement and pride. It was a joyful early language experience.

The children were very engaged while learning about how communication changed over time-from cave paintings to letters delivered on horseback, to Morse Code, telephones, and beyond. They eagerly shared what interested them most, with some fascinated by the Morse Code machine and others by early mail delivery. We also learned that people communicate in many ways: through Braille, sign language, and gestures. The children loved creating their own classroom sign language inspired by ASL or invented to meet their needs.
He applies his knowledge of the long A sound to accurately read and spell words with a_e, ai, and ay.

During our read-aloud of "What Do They Do With All That Poo?" the children were very curious about what a lion's "territory" means. They asked thoughtful questions (what "territory" means, what the lion does if another animal enters it), made connections to animals they know (like the dog who pees when outside of the yard), and listened carefully to each other's ideas. They showed growing skills in turn-taking, asking questions, and staying engaged during group discussions.

We visited the greenhouse to water the plants, and the everybody was highly engaged throughout the activity. They carefully helped fill the large watering can and then took turns filling their own smaller ones. They showed attention to detail, making sure not to miss any plants and ensuring each one received water.

While designing and building the crane prototypes, the children identified shapes, selected materials, and problem-solved challenges such as friction and balance. They observed how the string could prevent the roll from turning and how the paper cup needed to remain perpendicular to avoid spilling.

While learning traditional weather sayings, each child chose one and drew what it might look like. This helped them explore figurative language (like "flowers smell best before rain" or "a ring around the moon means rain is coming"). The children asked questions, explained their drawings, and enjoyed interpreting the sayings through art and storytelling.

The children were very involved in helping Robert pick up the pots that had fallen over after a small incident. Everyone organized themselves in such a way that they contributed to the cleanup without needing my intervention. They brought a mop, clothes, and even watered the plants afterwards. Some asked Robert if he was scared or how he was feeling. It was a great opportunity for communication and teamwork. Afterwards, we all reflected on what we had learned and observed, which led to some fruitful insights.

He was engaged and curious throughout the kickoff event. He showed great interest while identifying different seeds, carefully examining their shapes and sizes. It was wonderful to see him so invested in the learning process!

In our Crew Meet this week, the children participated in role-playing exercises to practice assertive communication in social interactions. They acted out scenarios such as sharing toys, responding to unkind behavior, and dealing with peer pressure. The focus was on helping them express their observations, feelings, and needs clearly and respectfully, empowering them to respond confidently and kindly in these situations.

After studying the properties of matter, the children were able to identify and analyze the properties of various materials to determine which would be best suited for constructing an interactive playground-one of the key objectives of our learning expedition. They evaluated different materials for outdoor use, recognizing that treated wood is durable and strong, metal is both durable and waterproof, plastic is waterproof, and rocks are highly durable.

During the activity where children chose one or two jobs they would like to do, everybody stayed focused and engaged as they carefully used scissors to cut out and stick their image, along with tools related to their chosen job, onto a piece of paper.

In ELA today, the children practiced using the double consonants "ff," "ss," and "ll" in various words. They engaged in activities such as word sorting and spelling games to identify and understand how these consonants function in different contexts. By the end of the lesson, they demonstrated improved spelling skills and confidence in recognizing these patterns in their reading and writing.

"During our weather discussion, the children explored how people used to understand weather before modern technology. They showed curiosity and made thoughtful guesses. When learning about tools like the rain gauge and anemometer, they quickly connected the information to real-life situations, explaining that knowing the amount of rain could help us care for plants or warn us about floods. The children watched instructional videos and then created their own weather instruments. They were excited to take them outside and observe the anemometer spinning in the wind. We will check the rain gauge again soon (our first version tipped over in the wind). This project showed their growing scientific thinking, teamwork, and eagerness to explore the natural world." 8

When introduced to the feeling puppets, the children responded with gentleness and care. They understood right away that "Scared" needed quiet voices, "Sad" needed hugs and kindness, and "Surprised" was fun to interact with. They waited patiently for their turns and showed empathy through their actions.

The children were excited to learn about bees during our guest visit and showed great curiosity by asking thoughtful questions such as, "How many bees are in a hive?" and "How do bees make honey?" Their engagement and eagerness to understand more about bees reflected their growing interest in the natural world.

Throughout our unit on plot structure and the elements of a story, the learners demonstrated a strong grasp of how a well-developed narrative unfolds. They analyzed story elements with increasing depth and were able to explain how the exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution work together. When creating their own narratives, they applied this understanding effectively, producing clear and well-structured Plot Mountain stories.

Children created an interactive ear puzzle model using conductive tape to build a simple circuit. They worked through drafts and revisions, testing their designs and making improvements along the way. Their first model did not work because the pieces were not cut accurately and the contact points did not connect properly, so they refined their work and adjusted the design. Once the components were placed in the correct order and the circuit was complete, the LED lit up in the auditory part of the brain, demonstrating how sound signals travel to be processed.

The children showed strong curiosity and engagement while exploring how a pulley system works. Using simple materials such as a rolling pin, string, and paper cup, they acted as engineers to investigate how pulling the string creates movement and allows objects to move up and down. This hands-on exploration supported their understanding of cause and effect, force, and motion

During our crew meeting this morning, we played a game to encourage teamwork. Initially, the boys became upset about not winning and directed frustration toward the girls. There was also a challenge with one team member not being fully involved, which affected the flow of the game. After a group discussion about teamwork, contributing to group success, and being a good sport, the boys reflected on their behavior. They stood up, shook each girl's hand, and said, "Good game." It was a great step toward understanding sportsmanship and collaboration.

In our activity on regrowing vegetables, children learned that nearly spoiled vegetables can grow new leaves when placed in water and light. They were fully engaged and excited to check daily how much the leaves had grown, demonstrating curiosity and understanding of what plants need to survive.

Today in ELA, the children practiced reading both leveled books and those assigned to them in Active Learn by blending the sounds we've studied. This activity is helping them improve their reading fluency and comprehension skills.

The children have been preparing a presentation for the Transform Event, focusing on the journey of their project. Today in ELA, we focused on practicing writing words using the sounds we have studied. The children received guidance on how to respect the lines and maintain proper spacing between words, helping them improve their writing skills.

Upon returning, the children shared their observations about the crosswalk they had designed and discussed improvements to make it more effective.

We practiced naming colors, which most children recognized easily. Naming them in English needed a bit more practice, but they were excited to learn. All of the learners mentioned took the initiative and started cleaning up the classroom. They organized the resources, used the broom, arranged the tables, and informed me about what was needed. I saw them diligently cleaning and taking responsibility for their school environment.

"The children worked in teams to build bridges for animals using cardboard, plastic, and paper. They experimented, problem-solved, and learned through trial and error. Together we discovered that: - Plastic bags need extra support to stay in place. - Paper becomes much stronger when folded in zigzags. - Cardboard is sturdy but not water-resistant. - Wrapping cardboard in plastic makes a strong outdoor bridge material. This was an excellent hands-on STEM learning experience full of teamwork and creative thinking." 7

The children enjoyed a rhythm-based number game that introduced using fingers as tools for addition. They laughed, tapped, and participated with enthusiasm. When asked to show a number with their fingers, some needed support but were eager to try again. This activity helped them connect movement, rhythm, and early math concepts.

"The group is working on a project about Food Waste, where they need to cover the following points: What does food waste mean? General data about this concept How we can prevent it Why prevention is important Suggestions for reducing food waste Each team member must have a specific role to ensure good organization. Before starting the project, they went through a research phase, where they read, extracted information, and discussed their findings. The requirement was for each team member to have detailed notes in their journal regarding their tasks and the information needed for the project, to keep track of what needs to be done. However, during this session, they focused on glueing two sheets of paper together, but none had glue-despite it being their responsibility. Additionally, one team member was absent, and that student had all the notes in their journal. This led to wasted time searching for information because they had not followed instructions from the beginning. We had a discussion to reflect on this experience and the lessons learned. This situation highlights the importance of planning and accountability in group work. The students need to anticipate challenges, such as missing materials or absent teammates, and develop a backup strategy instead of relying too much on one person. Encouraging them to distribute responsibilities more evenly and double-check resources before starting the work will help them become more independent and efficient in the future." 4

After a slow start playing the Urban Game on Thursday and a couple of discussions with the whole crew about attitudes toward the learning process, the Lower Middle crew learned their lesson. On Friday, they took the game seriously and we restarted with different teams. The game is based on a map formed by two A3 sheets. On those sheets, learners should draw a village from 1750 England following a specific set of instructions. The game has 20 rounds, and each round has a different story about the village. At the end of each round, learners should add or destroy buildings according to the instructions. The goal of the game is to teach learners how fast a community could change (in 100 years) with the help of inventions or other significant events from people's lives. Thus, on Thursday, Sofia was bored by the activity and she almost quit the game. Emi played it alone, Robert did what he could in Inoke's team and Andrei was really annoyed by the rules. On Friday, things changed. Sofia was more involved in the game and she demonstrated that she can be more focused when needed. Andrei and Robert joined the same team and they collaborated well and showed they could enjoy the activity. I saw them really focused at some points. Emi joined Sofia and Inoke and was active enough to place her own part on the team. All learners mentioned proved that they can learn from a historical game and that they can change their attitude and behaviour after the slideslip on Thursday.

"Tribe Meet brought the learners together in a new format within their own crew. The Red Ants were tasked with thinking of a game they could create from scratch. "After a slow start on their final project, Luca, Levi, and Marc worked together for two weeks to complete it. The struggle began early, as each of them had different ideas. We had many discussions but couldn't reach a meaningful conclusion. Therefore, I involved their parents in the discussion. After brief talks at home, all three learners came up with an idea inspired by their findings and by the resources provided in class. They illustrated a dual world. Using cardboard, they built one side representing an ideal, greener world based on renewable energy, forests, and eco-houses, and another side showing the world of tomorrow if humans take no action to restore environmental equilibrium. The grim future world was dominated by pollution, plastic waste scattered everywhere, and dead ecosystems and species. During those two weeks, Marc acted as the team leader. He guided Levi, and together they worked on the hands-on project, while Luca joined them later after preparing a Kahoot quiz for the parents with questions about climate change. Moreover, Marc prepared a speech about the greenhouse effect, and Levi took the lead in presenting their model in front of the parents. In the end, I saw great progress in this team, especially during the final week-with Marc as a confident leader, Levi working hard and pushing his limits in hands-on activities, and Luca stepping out of his comfort zone." 3

Darian can identify the three prehistoric ages: the Stone Age, Bronze Age, and Iron Age. He knows the Stone Age is divided into the Paleolithic, Mesolithic, and Neolithic periods. "Paleolithic" means "ancient stone," "Mesolithic" means "middle stone," and "Neolithic" means "new stone." During the Paleolithic era, people lived in caves or outdoors; in the Mesolithic era, they lived in tents made from animal skins; and in the Neolithic era, they began caring for animals and growing crops. People in the prehistoric era didn't have permanent housing and moved from place to place.

After a slow start playing the Urban Game on Thursday and a couple of discussions with the whole crew about attitudes toward the learning process, the Lower Middle crew learned their lesson. On Friday, they took the game seriously and we restarted with different teams. The game is based on a map formed by two A3 sheets. On those sheets, learners should draw a village from 1750 England following a specific set of instructions. The game has 20 rounds, and each round has a different story about the village. At the end of each round, learners should add or destroy buildings according to the instructions. The goal of the game is to teach learners how fast a community could change (in 100 years) with the help of inventions or other significant events from people's lives. Thus, on Thursday Levi took the lead of his team, but he was not able to cooperate efficiently with his team. A conflict sparked, and on Friday he joined another team, with Marcus, Yannick and Marc. In this new team, Levi was not a leader anymore, but I saw him contributing to the well-being of the team. He seemed to have fun during the game, but he was also being distracted by Yannick and Marc. Overall, I see that Levi needs to work more on his cooperation skills in order to fit in with other teams and to be more comfortable accepting different roles in the team. But, when he is concentrated, he can be a great asset to his team.
